quiz.title: Unit 5 Quiz 4, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Polyatomic Ions: Naming and Formulas
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Polyatomic Ions, Naming Ionic Compounds with Polyatomic Ions, Formula Writing, Oxyanions, Common Polyatomic Ions

questions.question: Name $\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3$:
questions.explanation: $\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3$ is named potassium carbonate. It contains the carbonate ion ($\text{CO}_3^{2-}$). Ionic compounds do not use prefixes, so dipotassium carbonate (C) is incorrect. Potassium carbonite (B) would be $\text{K}_2\text{CO}_2$, and potassium oxide (D) is $\text{K}_2\text{O}$.

questions.question: Which is the formula for calcium nitrate?
questions.explanation: Calcium nitrate is $\text{Ca(NO}_3)_2$. Calcium has a +2 charge ($\text{Ca}^{2+}$) and nitrate is $\text{NO}_3^-$ with a -1 charge, so two nitrates are needed. $\text{Ca(NO}_2)_2$ (D) would be calcium nitrite.

questions.question: Name $\text{Mg}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2$:
questions.explanation: $\text{Mg}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2$ is named magnesium phosphate. It contains the phosphate ion ($\text{PO}_4^{3-}$). Magnesium only forms one type of ion (+2), so no Roman numeral is needed. Magnesium phosphite (B) would contain $\text{PO}_3^{3-}$.

questions.question: Name $\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3$:
questions.explanation: $\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3$ is named ammonium nitrate. It contains two polyatomic ions: ammonium ($\text{NH}_4^+$) and nitrate ($\text{NO}_3^-$). Ammonia nitrate (C) is incorrect because ammonia is $\text{NH}_3$, not $\text{NH}_4^+$.

questions.question: Which formula is magnesium hydroxide?
questions.explanation: Magnesium hydroxide is $\text{Mg(OH)}_2$. Magnesium has a +2 charge ($\text{Mg}^{2+}$) and hydroxide is $\text{OH}^-$ with a -1 charge, so two hydroxides are needed. $\text{MgO}$ (D) is magnesium oxide.

questions.question: Name $\text{Ba}_3\text{N}_2$:
questions.explanation: $\text{Ba}_3\text{N}_2$ is named barium nitride. It is an ionic compound containing the nitride ion ($\text{N}^{3-}$). Barium only forms one type of ion (+2), so no Roman numeral is needed, and prefixes are not used for ionic compounds.

questions.question: Name $\text{CO}$:
questions.explanation: $\text{CO}$ is named carbon monoxide. It is a covalent compound, so prefixes are used. The 'mono-' prefix indicates one oxygen atom. Carbon dioxide (C) would be $\text{CO}_2$. Carbon (II) oxide (D) is an older, less common name.

questions.question: Name $\text{KClO}_3$:
questions.explanation: $\text{KClO}_3$ is named potassium chlorate. It contains the chlorate ion ($\text{ClO}_3^-$). Potassium chlorite (A) would be $\text{KClO}_2$, potassium hypochlorite (C) would be $\text{KClO}$, and potassium perchlorate (D) would be $\text{KClO}_4$.

questions.question: What is the name of $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$?
questions.explanation: $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$ is named carbonate. It is a polyatomic ion with a 2- charge. Carbonite (A) would be $\text{CO}_2^{2-}$ (which is not a common ion). Carboxylate (C) refers to a functional group in organic chemistry ($\text{RCOO}^-$). Carbon oxide (D) would refer to neutral compounds like $\text{CO}$ or $\text{CO}_2$.

questions.question: $\text{NH}_3$ is known as:
questions.explanation: $\text{NH}_3$ is commonly known as ammonia. It is a covalent compound with the systematic name nitrogen trihydride (C), but the common name ammonia is widely accepted and used. Ammonium (A) is $\text{NH}_4^+$, the polyatomic cation. Nitrous acid (D) is $\text{HNO}_2$.

questions.question: $\text{NH}_4^+$ is known as:
questions.explanation: $\text{NH}_4^+$ is known as ammonium. It is a polyatomic cation with a +1 charge. Ammonia (A) is $\text{NH}_3$, the neutral molecule. Nitrogen tetrahydride (C) is not a standard name for this ion. Amide (D) is $\text{NH}_2^-$ or refers to organic compounds with $-\text{CONH}_2$ groups.

questions.question: Name $\text{HBrO}$ (oxyacid):
questions.explanation: $\text{HBrO}$ is named hypobromous acid. The pattern for bromine oxyacids follows the anion names: $\text{BrO}^-$ (hypobromite) → hypobromous acid, $\text{BrO}_2^-$ (bromite) → bromous acid, $\text{BrO}_3^-$ (bromate) → bromic acid. Hydrobromic acid (A) would be $\text{HBr}$ (binary acid).

questions.question: Which of the following is a polyatomic ion?
questions.explanation: $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ (sulfate) is a polyatomic ion — a charged species composed of two or more atoms covalently bonded together. $\text{Na}^+$, $\text{Cl}^-$, and $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ are all monatomic ions (single atoms with a charge).

questions.question: What is the name of $\text{NO}_3^-$?
questions.explanation: $\text{NO}_3^-$ is named nitrate. Nitrite (A) is $\text{NO}_2^-$. Nitride (C) is $\text{N}^{3-}$. Nitrogen oxide (D) would refer to neutral compounds like $\text{NO}$ or $\text{NO}_2$.

questions.question: What is the correct formula for sodium nitrate?
questions.explanation: Sodium nitrate is $\text{NaNO}_3$. Sodium has a +1 charge ($\text{Na}^+$) and nitrate is $\text{NO}_3^-$ with a -1 charge, so they combine in a 1:1 ratio. $\text{NaNO}_2$ (A) would be sodium nitrite, and $\text{Na}_3\text{N}$ (D) would be sodium nitride.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered naming and formulas with polyatomic ions! 🧪📝
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review common polyatomic ions and their charges.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on distinguishing between -ate and -ite endings.
scoreMessages.0: Please review polyatomic ion names, formulas, and how to combine them with metals to form ionic compounds.
